  				vsuvorov
            	2023-05-19T15:13:35.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	How about this:

RUN off the CRUD.

RUN stands for Read , Update,  Nullify. Why is it preferred ('run off') over the CRUD (Create, Update, Delete)?

Consider the need to protect 'authentic data' in a decentralized environment.

In a decentralized control model, the data always originates from a controller (aka client). The data created

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
(sourced) by the controller follows the principle of 'Non-Interactive Replay Monotonicity' to be able to protect the data from a replay (events are changed) or a deletion (some events are deleted) attacks. That is to say, the data (or events comprising it) is never deleted, it's rather always added to via updates. Each update, therefore, forms a verifiable, continuous log ( e.g. by providing growing sequence number, date timestamp, etc for each update).  To enable invalidation of data, a special update, called Nullify, is used.

The client, therefore, updates the server (it's peer or peers), which just maintains the log following certain rules (see BADA - Best Available Data Acceptance).

To summarise, the server can only Read the log, add Updates to it, including Nullifying ones. So no Create or Delete.

								nuttawut.kongsuwan
								2023-05-26T18:08:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Here is my mental model for KERI watchers, which may not be accurate.

I would like to separate two cases: (1) controllers are typical end users and (2) controllers are organizations that issue credentials.

For end users, watchers are probably unnecessary most of the time. Alice can send a KEL to Bob and another inconsistent KEL to Cedric without causing any problems. Duplicity would arise only in the case where Bob and Cedric need to exchange their data about Alice. In this scenario, either Bob or Cedric may set up watchers to monitor Alice’s witnesses.

For organizations, they may issue a lot of credentials (ACDCs) to many people, organizations, or even to the public (as untargeted ACDCs). In this case, I believe it is necessary to set up watchers.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								nuttawut.kongsuwan
								2023-05-26T18:08:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								I can give an extreme example where a government is behaving duplicitously. In this case, the government’s witnesses are designed to promulgate different KELs to different groups of people.

Let’s say the president of that country issues an (untargeted) ACDC and makes it available to half of its citizens using one version of its KEL. Then, the

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
president uses another version of its KEL that is inconsistent with the previous one to issue another ACDC that is made available to the other half of its citizens.

Without watchers, the citizens might not be able to detect that two inconsistent KELs have been used. The government may also repudiate one of its ACDCs at a later time.

If watchers are available, on the other hand, the citizens will be able to tell immediately that the government is doing something fishy.

								vsuvorov
								2023-05-26T18:08:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Hmm. Controllers publish their witness sets ( OOBI). Each witness maintains a hash linked, witness and controller signed "micro ledger" (aka KEL). Assuming that witnesses are not compromised ( RUN and not CRUD) the duplicity originates from the controller ( it signs all the events in the chain, collects and distributes the witness receipts). In other words, Alice would need to construct completely different versions of the KEL and get its set of witnesses to re-write the history for duplicity to reach the public. If Alice, on the other hand, publishes different sets of witnesses ( not via rotation - it retains history)  then it means that there are two version of the Alice's identity in the wild. But these two versions are consistent ( because of the write only KELs).
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-05-26T18:08:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Watchers can be used to both detect rewritten history (compromised or incorrect witnesses) and verify identity's messages and ACDCs.
Any agent that has to deal with KERI identities ( same as prefixes) has to maintain a local copy of the identity's KEL and watch out for rotation ( rot + drt) and interaction (ixn) events in order to maintain the latest set of keys, backers ( aka witnesses) sets, anchors for delegated identities and TELs.

While doing so, watcher must obtain the logs on periodic basis and compare them against the stored and verified KEL ( first time obtained via OOBI). Store the difference. Due to chaining ( sn + p, p being said of the prev event) any attempt to rewrite history will be detected. At this poin, a watcher can poll other witnesses in the set and thus detect "lying" witnesses or determine that prefix is not trust worthy ( assuming the first OOBI check was performed agains all witnesses).

Furthermore, one can use "first seen replay couples" ( sn + timestamp) to determine the moment in time when the attempts to rewrite history started.

            	I really like the goal of leveraging “trust that is cheap, common, and neglected by heavier-weight mechanisms”

Adding biometric hashes and confirmed handles, as well as  the “basis” field and “caveats” seems too complicated to get started. Great roadmap features, yet so much optionality there, and difficult to constrain without knowing how to limit it well.

I suggest starting with only four attributes. knownAs, minutes, dt (timestamp), and a very constrained “basis” field so that it is easy to use and hard to confuse.

Since the goal is to establish personhood then online handles aren’t really necessary or can be condensed into the knownAs field as a separate credential for each online handle. why not use a multiplicity of credentials? If you do not mind, I have some dumb questions due to my lack of understanding of how metadata works in typical opaquely-structured-data.

• When an opaquely-structured-data file is saidified, is its metadata also hashed along with its content?  — I suppose it is a yes.
• When the SAID is embedded in the filename “q2financials-E2948372a9b9019.docx”, if the filename is changed to, e.g., “report-E2948372a9b9019.docx”, is the SAID still valid? — My understanding is that the SAID must be recomputed if the filename is changed. 
• If I understand correctly, the metadata of a file changes when you use it in different operating systems. Would this cause a problem with the saidification if the files must be used in a multi-OS environment?
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								daniel.hardman

								Hi, <@U04H17ZEX9R>. Believe it or not, I just noticed this now. Sorry to be 2 months la

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
te. Wow.

Yes, metadata stored *inside* the file is hashed along with content. But metadata stored *outside* the file (e.g., last mod date, permissions) is NOT hashed.

When SAID is embedded in filename, the SAID does *not* have to be recomputed when the filename changes. The filename is not input to the hash function. Rather, the regex for the filename that's stored inside the file is input to the hash -- and that regex doesn't change when the filename changes. For example, if the regex is `.*{said}\.docx`, then any filename containing the SAID and ending in docx is a valid filename, and nothing needs to be recomputed when the file is renamed.

Re. multi-OS: no, this metadata is not hashed and would not cause a problem.

The payment for a Slack workspace (a collection of channels, direct messages, and the people who belong to them) is typically managed by the workspace owner or an admin. If you want to pay for a Slack workspace that was set up by someone else, here are some general steps you could follow:
1. Contact the Workspace Owner/Admin: Reach out to the workspace owner or an admin to express your intention. They can change billing responsibilities if they agree.
2. Change Billing Ownership: If the current workspace owner or admin agrees, they can transfer ownership to you. This can be done through the workspace

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
settings. Remember, this gives you control over the entire workspace, not just the payment, so it’s a significant responsibility.
3. Add a New Payment Method: Once you’re the billing owner, you can add a new payment method. Go to 
4. S`ettings & administration`
5.  > `Billing` > `Payment method`.
6. Update the Payment Information: Enter your payment information and make sure it’s set as the default. You will then be billed for the workspace subscription.
Remember, it’s important to discuss these changes with the current workspace owner/admin and other relevant parties to ensure everyone is comfortable with the change. If you’re paying on behalf of a company, it’s also essential to make sure this complies with any relevant policies or procedures your company may have.
Please note that this information is based on Slack’s policies and procedures as of September 2021, so it may have changed. Be sure to check Slack’s current documentation or contact their support for the most accurate and up-to-date information.

ChatGPT
The payment for a Slack workspace (a collection of channels, direct messages, and the people who belong to them) is typically managed by the workspace owner or an admin. If you want to pay for a Slack workspace that was set up by someone else, here are some general steps you could follow:
1. Contact the Workspace Owner/Admin: Reach out to the workspace owner or an admin to express your intention. They can change billin

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
g responsibilities if they agree.
2. Change Billing Ownership: If the current workspace owner or admin agrees, they can transfer ownership to you. This can be done through the workspace settings. Remember, this gives you control over the entire workspace, not just the payment, so it’s a significant responsibility.
3. Add a New Payment Method: Once you’re the billing owner, you can add a new payment method. Go to 
4. S`ettings & administration`
5.  > `Billing` > `Payment method`.
6. Update the Payment Information: Enter your payment information and make sure it’s set as the default. You will then be billed for the workspace subscription.
Remember, it’s important to discuss these changes with the current workspace owner/admin and other relevant parties to ensure everyone is comfortable with the change. If you’re paying on behalf of a company, it’s also essential to make sure this complies with any relevant policies or procedures your company may have.
Please note that this information is based on Slack’s policies and procedures as of September 2021, so it may have changed. Be sure to check Slack’s current documentation or contact their support for the most accurate and up-to-date information.

As a start I began exploring possibility of strong compile-time typings for some of the primitives like identifiers. I quickly ran into TS limitations. For example it's possible to type for a 44 character base64 SAID but it fails with depth of recursion for 512 (88 char) identifiers. We could use Opaque types but it's a bit hacky with TS and you make some downstream trade-off.

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
for developers
We have lawyers, ux designers, musicians, philanthropists, a wide range of "non-technical" (I dislike this term but it works for now) people who contribute and want to be part of this community.

Selecting a tool which would provide the easiest and broadest support to our diverse group is of extremely high value to me.
Migrating to something with a familiar interface, server/workspace channel structure would maintain accessibility for the whole community and maybe even a tool they already use, not an additional one.

I'd be incredibly sad to think an artist or product manager, felt they couldn't engage with the community because they did not feel "savvy" enough.

As others may point out, this could all be moot anyway, this was the first mention and we there are no concrete plans in the community.

content:
dational than SSI. One can use KERI/ACDC to build either SSI or non-SSI applications/ecosystems.

From the technical standpoint, the core value of KERI/ACDC is in “Authenticity”, that is, to build something with strong secure attribution. Other properties like privacy, confidentiality, and anonymity are important too, but they are not the top priority.

This is quite different from the core value of SSI which emphasizes self-sovereignty, control, and ownership of personal data for individuals.

However, the technical value may or may not reflect the values of members of the community. This might be a good philosophical discussion for the community.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								robert.mitwicki
								2023-08-10T16:56:42.000Z
							
							
							    
								I can share same view from perspective of Human Colossus that protocols behind KERI and ACDC address low level architecture of authenticity in general. We have many applications where those protocols have nothing to do with SSI. It does not mean that you cannot build SSI application but we cannot ignore the existing world which is not SSI driven and won't be for very long time.

In our case we leveraging properties of KERI to provide integrity (SAID) and authenticity (SCID)  to lower layers of dynamic data economy stack, You can read more about that in this blog post:  and in referenced in that blog post document about DDE 

not many people understand bigger picture of accurate data

								Anonymity is not as big of a core value in this community as it is in other cryptography communities where zero knowledge proofs are king.

Maybe the KERI & ACDC community can get a custom contract with Discord that respects chain link confidentiality.     			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				daniel.hardman
            	2023-08-16T20:55:17.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	Hi, <@U04H17ZEX9R>. Believe it or not, I just noticed this now. Sorry to be 2 months late. Wow.

Yes, metadata stored *inside* the file is hashed along with content. But metadata stored *outside* the file (e.g., last mod date, permissions) is NOT hashed.

When SAID is embedded in filename, the SAID does *not* have to be recomputed when the filename changes. The filename is not input to the hash function. Rather, the regex for the filename that's stored inside the file is input to the hash -- and that regex doesn't change when the filename changes. For example, if the regex is `.*{said}\.docx`, then any filename containing the SAID and ending in docx is a valid filename, and nothing needs to be recomputed when the file is renamed.

Re. multi-OS: no, this metadata is not hashed and would not cause a problem.

    			Number of replies: 4
				
						
							
								
								daniel.hardman
								2023-09-06T09:50:40.000Z
							
							
							    
								In Provenant's current command-line wallets, we run `kli local watch` every time we log in. This helps the different members of some of our multisig AIDs catch up if they've missed events since their last login. It works well.

However, I'd like an efficient way to do some more ambitious watching than this -- and that's what this thread is about.

Thing 1 that I'd like to do: `register a webhook with a witness so I can be called back any time ANY KEL/TEL changes`

Thing 2 that I'd like to do: `poll a witness efficiently for all changes in any KEL/TEL since timestamp or state hash X`

I am wondering if either of these things is already supported. If not, <@U024CJMG22J> or <@U024KC347B4>, do you think would the community accept a PR from Provenant adding either or both of these features?

Unlike the `kli local watch` command, the most convenient scope of both of these features would be "all the AIDs the witness services" rather than an enumerated subset of AIDs.

Regarding #1: I don't imagine that registering the webhook has to be exposed to the public over a web service endpoint; I'm rather imagining this as something configured internally by the owner/

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
operator of a witness. Provenant would use it on its own witnesses, because it would make it very easy to discover that a credential has been issued or revoked, or a key has been rotated.

Regarding #2: I'm imagining this would happen over a web service endpoint exposed by the witness, and that it could potentially be called by the public (albeit with optional enforcement of some kind of proof of authorization). Provenant would love to call GLEIF's witnesses periodically over an API like this, making sure that we find out about any noteworthy changes rooted in those witnesses. With this feature, if a scope of "all the AIDs the witness services" is deemed too broad, it could still be useful for the smaller scope of an enumerated set of AIDs.

It is possible that this second feature is the logical inverse of the witness gossip feature that's been long imagined (pull vs. push), and that witness gossip could be implemented atop it (or at least with a lot of common code).

Provenant doesn't need these features in the next 6 weeks, but we probably do need them before the end of the year, so if it's something we need to work on, I'd love to be taught/pointed in the right direction and start the journey. If someone has already implemented part or all of this, or is in the process of doing so, so much the better.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-09-06T09:50:40.000Z
							
							
							    
								There is no support for callbacks coming out of witnesses.  That would be a net new feature.

There is a fairly extensive query API exposed from witnesses (as a matter of fact, `kli local watch` uses it) to support query for current state of an AID, current log of an AID and with parameters like, `sn=?` and `anchor={...}` which will live in the witness until that condition is met or until a timeout is reached.  All of these work using a KERI `qry` event and when satisfied, the witness sends back a `rpy` event, so they are asynchronous, not an HTTP interactive API.  Also, they all work on only one AID at a time.

If you want to see how it all works, you should start in `Kevery.processQuery` in `eventing.py` in KERIpy.  That is called from the parser when a `qry` event is streamed in.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								daniel.hardman
								2023-09-06T09:50:40.000Z
							
							
							    
								I've spent some time studying. My unfamiliarity with the codebase is a handicap, but I m

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
ade some progress.

Let me clarify why I want this.

Provenant imagines supporting high-speed signing for millions of brands. These brands will all have a multisig AID, and they will delegate signing authority to a single-sig AID that can be operated by automation at machine speed. Signing an SMS message or similar does not generate an ACDC and is not anchored to the KEL, because the signatures are only relevant for a short lifecycle (e.g., 10 seconds).

All of these brands will want to be able to revoke delegated signing authority with low latency (e.g., a few seconds of reaction time). This means that Provenant needs to know if delegated authority was changed on millions of AIDs, within a few seconds of when it happens. I don't believe it will be practical to monitor individual KELs and poll for changes.

The good news is, 99.9% of these brands should be using Provenant witnesses. If we configure our own witnesses to tell us when the KEL of an AID changes (the new feature I want, not callable over public web interface), we can then call the witness back and get an updated version of the KEL (existing witness feature), very soon after the change occurs.  Since most KELs are unlikely to change frequently, we will have the efficiency we need.

It seems to me that what I want might best be hooked up to KeyStateNoticer and WitnessInquisitor in some way. Am I in the right ballpark?
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								daniel.hardman
								2023-09-06T09:50:40.000Z
							
							
							    
								Actually, maybe processEvent in Kevery?

Let me clarify why I want this.

Provenant imag

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
ines supporting high-speed signing for millions of brands. These brands will all have a multisig AID, and they will delegate signing authority to a single-sig AID that can be operated by automation at machine speed. Signing an SMS message or similar does not generate an ACDC and is not anchored to the KEL, because the signatures are only relevant for a short lifecycle (e.g., 10 seconds).

All of these brands will want to be able to revoke delegated signing authority with low latency (e.g., a few seconds of reaction time). This means that Provenant needs to know if delegated authority was changed on millions of AIDs, within a few seconds of when it happens. I don't believe it will be practical to monitor individual KELs and poll for changes.

The good news is, 99.9% of these brands should be using Provenant witnesses. If we configure our own witnesses to tell us when the KEL of an AID changes (the new feature I want, not callable over public web interface), we can then call the witness back and get an updated version of the KEL (existing witness feature), very soon after the change occurs.  Since most KELs are unlikely to change frequently, we will have the efficiency we need.

It seems to me that what I want might best be hooked up to KeyStateNoticer and WitnessInquisitor in some way. Am I in the right ballpark?

            	From my research (using chatGPT):
To download images and videos from a Slack workspace, which are not included in the standard workspace history export, you can follow these steps:
1. Use Slack’s API: Slack’s API provides endpoints for accessing files uploaded to your workspace. You can use methods like `files.list` to retrieve a list of file URLs. This requires some programming knowledge and an understanding of working with APIs.
2. Manual Download: If there aren’t many files or if you’re looking for specific ones, you can manually download them. Go to the conversation or channel where the file was shared and click on the file to download it.
3. Third-party Tools: There are third-party tools and services available that can help you download all files from your Slack workspace. These tools usually integrate with Slack and automate the process of downloading files. Be sure to choose a reputable and secure service, as this involves sensitive data.
4. Request Slack Support: If you’re facing difficulties or if your workspace has a large number of files, consider contacting Slack support for assistance. They may be able to provide a solution tailored to your specific needs.
5. Automate with a Script: If you have programming skills, you can write a script using Slack’s API to automate the download process. This could be especially useful if there are a large number of files.
Even though I could go for option 2, I’m not interested to manually reconstruct it this way.

Number of replies: 0
				

			
		

		
			
				
				pfeairheller
				2022-10-08T20:40:59.000Z
			

			
				Hi Rodolfo- We upgraded the SSE endpoint and the notifications recently to
					match more of a Firebase approach.

					Anything coming over the SSE channel is just a ping to a live client to refresh some other data set.
					The Notifications are a persistent dataset that are messages to the controller of an agent to take
					some action. They are persistent and need to be managed (marked as read, deleted) by the controller
					of the agent.

					So right now, the main use of SSE events is to let the controller of an agent know that there are
					new notifications and that a client should reload that dataset.

					Sadly we have not documented the SSE events yet. 
				
			
			
				
					Number of replies: 0

  				pfeairheller
            	2022-10-12T01:54:52.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	The OOBI is the mechanism to allow a controller to associate an endpoint with an AID.  It says, "to contact this AID, use this endpoint (tcp or http)".    Then any controller that has resolved the OOBI for a witness just needs to specify that witness by AID in it inception event.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2022-10-12T01:57:36.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	So client configuration needs to provide the witness OOBIs in keripy configuration to allow the AID -> endpoint association and the client needs to allow the controller of an agent to select the witness by AID.  In the Keep for the vLEI ecosystem we provide "pools" of witnesses (by AID) that are collections from the GLEIF provided witness network.  The OOBIs are in the agent configuration and the user interface allows the controller to select them by "pool" which puts the AID in the `b` field of the inception event.

            	2022-10-17T14:08:06.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	It all depends on what endpoints are exposed via OOBIs.  For all of our current samples (in the scripts directory) and our work with the vLEI, we are exposing witness OOBIs from every controller and controller OOBIs from every witnesses.  So when I resolve an OOBI for another, non-witness AID I receive the KEL of the controller which provides the cryptographic commitment to the witness and I receive reply messages with provides BADA-RUN data for endpoints for the witness (who has persistent availability on the internet and thus exposes both TCP and HTTP endpoints).  Because I do not have endpoints for the AID but I

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
do have endpoints for her witnesses, I have to send messages to the mailboxes on the witnesses to be forwarded to the AID controller.  Those messages can be sent to either the TCP or the HTTP endpoint on the witness but for now we are only using HTTP.

Since we are configuring out agents without exposed ports on the internet (or using the command line) we run all agents and command line commands with pollers for their mailboxes.  They check all witnesses for any messages that have been forwarded to their AIDs.  The pollers pull the messages down and send them through the parser just as if they arrived in any other manner (direct connection via TCP or over HTTP).

								As for `kli query` ...  we just really need watchers.  Once we have watchers, we can OOBI with another AID and then let our watcher know that we are doing business with that AID and to keep an eye on it.  Then we merely have to ask our watcher what the current key state is.

								pfeairheller

								To avoid any sort of eclipse attack, just add more watchers to make it exponentially harder for someone to successfully attack you.

            	It is looking like the meaning of what `base` may have changed over time. In the `commands/incept.py` the doc string states
> additional optional prefix to file location of KERI keystoreand `hio/base/filing.py` constructs the path as so:
path = os.path.abspath(
                        os.path.expanduser(
                            os.path.join(headDirPath,
                                         tailDirPath,
                                         base,
                                         name)))This led me to believe that the `base` attribute was originally intended to be used as an optional segment of the path like so:
`/headDirPath/tailDirPath/base/name`

Because of this code segment in `Filer` I assumed that both `headDirPath` and `base` must be specified together

However, the `existing.existingHby` context manager does not have a property for specifying the configuration directory, headDirPath, which leads me to believe base is being used with an assumption of using the default `HeadDirPath` or `AltHeadDirPath` from the `Keeper` class.

            	2022-12-28T00:57:35.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	This change ended up being bigger than I planned though to be consistent I made the change in all KLI commands that use `base` and `config_dir`.
Consistency was my main goal in this PR. If you want me to chop it up into multiple smaller PRs I’d be happy to.
It begs the question of whether the argument should be called `config_dir` or `headDirPath` since the underlying `Filer` implementation uses `headDirPath`.

It seems like having a common configuration approach for all of the CLI commands is useful so we can make assumptions across the whole CLI surface area.

Please take a look:

content:
r on  line while running the test.
FAILED test_configing.py::test_configer::136 - AssertionError: assert False*Findings:*
If the user has root permission `configing.Configer()` creates the file inside `/usr/local/var/keri/cf/main/conf.json`. And `configing.Configer(headDirPath="/root/keri")` creates the file inside `/root/keri/keri/cf/main/conf.json`.
But without the root permission, both `configing.Configer()` & `configing.Configer(headDirPath="/root/keri")` creates file inside `${HOME}/.keri/cf/main/conf.json`

 test works perfectly on github action & local environment(if doesn't have root permission). But doesn't work inside Docker(or environment having root permission) as the file created inside `/root/keri/keri/cf/main/conf.json`

            	2023-02-02T19:46:49.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	I’d like to write a blog post tutorial and video on how to do what would be `kli vc verify` so it is reasonably clear to anyone trying to use KERI how to use it for issuing and verifying credentials.

Here’s the high level overview from what I understand. Please correct me where I am wrong and add any missing parts.

I know Petteri mentioned  and it’s only a few hundred lines of code. I haven’t looked into it deeply yet.
From what I understand there are a few things involved:
1. Prefix creation on either side, both controllers A and B
2. pairwise OOBI exchange between controllers A and B
3. VC registry creation for controller A
4. ACDC schema creation by controller A
5. ACDC schema registration by controller A
6. ACDC issuance by controller A
7. ACDC transmission to controller B, which likely triggers a VC registry creation in the Hab if it doesn’t exist yet.
8. ACDC schema discovery by controller B
9. ACDC reception by controller B verifies the credential.
10. ACDC schema validation by controller B
11. ACDC signature verification by controller B
I haven’t looked deeply into the vLEI codebase yet though from what I can tell it is used for some sort of schema registration and discovery.

And if the features don’t exist to do this yet then please post the Github Issues for them so I can know where to focus my efforts. I’m ready to pitch in to build whatever is missing.

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
o understand what performs the execution of the following task:
def list_credentials(args):
    """ Command line list credential registries handler

    """
    ld = ListDoer(name=args.name,
                  alias=args.alias,
                  base=args.base,
                  bran=args.bran,
                  verbose=args.verbose,
                  poll=args.poll,
                  said=args.said,
                  issued=args.issued,
                  schema=args.schema)
    return [ld]Is the ListDoer invoked on instantiation or by something that executes all doers in the returned list?
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:27:40.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	There is a lot to learn here.  I'll give you a quick overview of what I've learned working with Hio through KERIpy.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:31:12.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	Doers are coroutines that will yield execution to other coroutines throughout Hio.  In particular, while waiting for I/O.  You create them by either extending the Doer class from hio or by creating a "generator method" (one that `yields`) in python and "doifying" it with `doing.doify`
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:31:32.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	A good example of this is all the methods in KERIpy named something like `escrowDo`.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:35:42.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	The `H` in Hio stands for "hierarchical" and so with Hio you can create a hierarchy of coroutines that manage each other and have dependencies.  The main class for creating a hierarchy is the `DoDoer` which is initialized with a list of dependent `Doers` .  A great example of this in KERIpy is any of the Endpoint classes in `kiwiing` that extend DoDoer, launch with something like a Postman and one of its own methods that needs to run as a coroutine, but also creates and manages new coroutines during its lifecycle.  For example, some will create `WitnessReceipter` instances that run as coroutines.  The Endpoint will create it, add it as a running coroutine with `self.extend` , let it perform its task, th

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
en clean up by removing it with `self.remove`   Lots of examples in kwiwing of this behavior.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:36:49.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	Finally, to start a tree of coroutines (Doers and DoDoers) you need a `Doist`.  That is the top level that runs an entire hierarchy.   Here is the `runController` method from KERIpy that is a helper for starting an entire hierarchy:

def runController(doers, expire=0.0):
    """
    Utiitity Function to create doist to run doers
    """
    tock = 0.03125
    doist = doing.Doist(limit=expire, tock=tock, real=True)
    (doers=doers)
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:37:31.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	That will run forever or until there are no more coroutines running, then it will exit.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:38:37.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	One of the challenges of the `kli` is to run and wait for coroutines to finish and clean up after they are done (with tons of asynchronous tasks going on in the background) and exit cleanly.  Most commands want to have a quick clean exit.  Others run forever (`kli start witness`).
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:39:47.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	If you look at `kli.py` you'll see that it treats all the subcommands as Doer factories which it loads and then calls `runController` allowing the program to run until all the Doers it was given are done.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-06T17:42:09.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	A common pattern in the kli command set is to have one top level class that is the `DoDoer` that starts all dependencies, run their coroutines, then have one method that is itself a coroutine that does all the work (including creating and deleting new coroutines), then cleans up the dependency coroutines with `self.remove` and exits leaving the top level Doist with nothing to do so it exits.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
an establishment only `estOnly` KEL use a rotation event to anchor the registry inception seal?
Lines 400-404 of `src/keri/vdr/credentialing.py`
        if not hab.group:
            if estOnly:
                hab.rotate(data=[rseal])
            else:
                hab.interact(data=[rseal])What is the thinking behind when a registry inception event is also an establishment event?
Why would it be different?
Is it that for establishment only KELs that registry inception is allowed in that registry creation can be seen as a type of establishment event, the establishment of a credential?
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				kent
            	2023-02-22T17:17:53.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	And, a follow up question, which was my original question:
Are interaction events the mechanism by which a credential’s TEL is connected to a prefix’s KEL? It seems I have answered my own question in the affirmative, though I want to make sure I’m not missing anything.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-22T17:43:47.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	You are looking at it backwards.  When you declare an AID to establishment only, you are only allowed inception and rotation events.  Therefore the ONLY way you can anchor anything into your KEL is with a rotation event because interaction events are not allowed.  This provides an added layer of security because your keys all become one time use keys for key events.  Once used, they have to be rotated because you are only allowed rotation events.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-02-22T17:46:50.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	The anchoring is not "connecting" the TEL but providing a cryptographic commitment to the TEL.  You are creating an event in your own KEL with the SAID of the TEL event and signing the event in your KEL, thus also signing the SAID of the TEL event, providing the commitment.  This applies whether the anchor is an interaction event or a rotation event.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				kent
            	2023-02-22T18:34:06.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	I see, so both a rotation event and an interaction event may be used to anchor cryptographic commitments in a KEL.

So a registry inception e

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
vent is not an establishment event. An establishment event, rotation event, can anchor a commitment to the TEL for the registry inception event.

When a given AID is establishment only this provides an additional layer of security by requiring a new key for each event, meaning a specific key used to anchor a TEL commitment will only be used once in an establishment only KEL.

Thank you for clearing this up.

								petteri.stenius
								2023-02-24T04:21:36.000Z
							
							
							    
								You can start multiple withess instances from a single shell.

On a single host, each witness instance must have a different name with `--name` parameter. They must also listen on different ports with `-H` and `-T` parameters.

For example I have used a script like this, to start the exact same witness `kli witness demo` starts. Put the following into wan.sh. Repeat with wil.sh, wes.sh etc, changing name, salt, tcp and http parameters in each.

```#!/bin/bash
cd ~/keripy/scripts || exit 1
. ./demo/demo-scripts.sh || exit 1
name=wan
salt=0AB3YW5uLXRoZS13aXRuZXNz
tcp=5632
http=5642
kli init --name $name --nopasscode --salt $salt
kli incept --name $name --alias=$name --config ${KERI_SCRIPT_DIR} --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/wil-witness-sample.json
exec kli witness start --name $name --alias=$name -T $tcp -H $http```
Then I start all witnesses with the following script. Put this into `witness-demo.sh`

```#!/bin/bash

function stop-all {
    kill $(jobs -p)
}

trap stop-all EXIT

bash wan.sh &
bash wil.sh &
bash wes.sh &

wait $(jobs -p)```
To start all witnesses run `bash witness-demo.sh`

  				petteri.stenius
            	2023-02-26T10:38:10.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	You can start multiple withess instances from a single shell.

On a single host, each witness instance must have a different name with `--name` parameter. They must also listen on different ports with `-H` and `-T` parameters.

For example I have used a script like this, to start the exact same witness `kli witness demo` starts. Put the following into wan.sh. Repeat with wil.sh, wes.sh etc, changing name, salt, tcp and http parameters in each.

#!/bin/bash
cd ~/keripy/scripts || exit 1
. ./demo/demo-scripts.sh || exit 1
name=wan
salt=0AB3YW5uLXRoZS13aXRuZXNz
tcp=5632
http=5642
kli init --name $name --nopasscode --salt $salt
kli incept --name $name --alias=$name --config ${KERI_SCRIPT_DIR} --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/wil-witness-sample.json
exec kli witness start --name $name --alias=$name -T $tcp -H $httpThen I start a

								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								Some of the descriptions here are inaccurate.  The OOBIs defined in the configuration under the key `iurls` are OOBIs for resolving key state.  They are not only witness OOBIs.  In addition, the OOBIs listed in the samples are also not specific to witnesses.  They are "controller" OOBIs because the are designating a resolution point for the controller (thus the role of 'controller' in the URL) key state and endpoints of the AID also listed in the URL.   That same type of URL will work for any AID, not just witnesses.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								I'm not going to discuss data OOBIs in this thread because I think it will muddy the water and not help solve the original question.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								So for the KERIpy samples, the controller OOBIs listed in the config files provide endpoint

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
and key state resolution for the witnesses themselves.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								So that when you want to use a one of the resolved AIDs as a witness, your instance will know how to contact the witness.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								The problem you are encountering has one of two possible causes.  First, the lack of the role on the URL ('controller') could very well be the problem.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								Second, if the witnesses are not launched correctly with config files that tell them what endpoints to expose, they will not reply with any endpoint information in response to the OOBI.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								That data is found in block named after the alias for the AID of the witness in the `curls`  block.  See here for the "wan" witness:

```{
  "dt": "2022-01-20T12:57:59.823350+00:00",
  "wan": {
    "dt": "2022-01-20T12:57:59.823350+00:00",
    "curls": ["", ""]
  },
  "iurls": [
  ]
}```
This config file for the witness launched with the name of "wan" is being told to generate endpoint responses for 2 endpoints, one HTTP and one TCP.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-02-27T14:53:22.000Z
							
							
							    
								So you need to ensure each witness has the correct configuration file for its ports and IP addresses and that they line up correctly with the ports and IP addresses the witnesses are listening on.

								kent
								2023-02-28T17:30:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								Hello Vasily,

the code is typically the best source if you’ve already read and absorbed all of the IETF Draft specs and the whitepaper.

For your specific question on the flow of events to and between witnesses I believe the whitepaper is the most up to date resource. The  has a set of messages in sections 7 through 12 though there isn’t a lot of prose explaining the protocol.

Communication is between the controller and the witnesses right now for KELs. There is no witness to witness communication at the moment though I believe this is planned. 								kent
								2023-03-01T23:14:35.000Z
							
							
							    
								Looks like I was wrong. It was the arguments I was passing in to the `kli init` command that were creating the file in `${CONFIG_DIR}` :
```kli init --name wan --salt 0AB3YW5uLXRoZS13aXRuZXNz --nopasscode --config-dir "${KERI_SCRIPT_DIR}" --config-file wan-witness```
I was missing a `main/` path segment on the `wan-witness` file I was using.
Due to the default arguments for the `Configer` class it uses the path segment `"main"` when looking for configuration files.
```class Configer(filing.Filer):
  ...
  TailDirPath = "keri/cf"
  ...
   def __init__(self, name="conf", base="main", filed=True, mode="r+b",
                 fext="json", human=True, **kwa):
    ...```
The problem can be worked around (solved?) by just putting a prefix on the path given to the `--config-file` argument as so:
```--config-file main/wan-witness```

								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								kent
								2023-03-01T23:14:35.000Z
							
							
							    
								There are two potential `--base` arguments though only one exists in the CLI interface for `kli witness start` right now.
1. The base directory for the keystore. (in the .keri or /usr/local dir)
2. The base directory for the configuration files. (set to $KERI_SCRIPTS_DIR in the sample scripts)

								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								kent
								2023-03-01T23:14:35.000Z
							
							
							    
								In the future we could separate them out with another change to the `kli witness start` command by allowing `--base-keystore` and `--base-config` options.

            	Let's say I'd like to verify a signature in a hotpath. What is the best way to make sure I'm using the latest public key? do I need to make a qry with a `/ksn` route to a witness? The other alternative is to use an oobi backend and get the whole KEL, but it's more involved ( and slower ?) as one needs to traverse the KEL.

The reason I'm asking is I'm thinking about usage of keri ids in access tokens, etc.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 6
				
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								In cases where a signature is attached to a KERI event, the keys used are the latest signing keys for that KEL.  In cases where a signature is attached to something _other than_ a KERI event, it is usually accompanied by a seal in an attachment that specifies the location in the KEL at which is was attached.  This way signatures can last beyond rotations.  If the signature is supposed to be short lived, the "latest" seal can be used to indicate "current key state".

If you are checking for a specific entry in a KEL, you can check your local cache to see if you have _at least_ that sequence number and get the keys from that point in the key state or query one of the AIDs witnesses for a specific sequence number.

If you look at `messagize` in `eventing.py` in KERIpy you can see the application of the two types of Seals mentioned above (inside the `if signers` block).

If you look at `WitnessInquisitor.query` method in `agenting.py` you can see the options for querying a witness for various conditions related to an AID's key state.  For example, you can query by sequence number or by anchor (used for delegation).
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								<@U024CJMG22J> - thanks a lot! That's really helpful. I'm looking at the situations where a signature will be attached to a non KERI related data. Such as an access to token, or an attribute inside a cert ( which can also carry

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
SPIFFE id and therefore be a SVID).

The way I was thinking about it is : I have a vLEI and a role, perhaps with a delegated key for IoT, or API access. After being 'onboarded' ( similar to to providing CA cert)  I can then lookup the current key ( short lived case) for the prefix and verify the signature. Or update the local cache periodically. Which means, I guess, that I'll need to attach not only the prefix but a seal, minimally an `sn`  of the corresponding establishment event.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								<@U024CJMG22J> - I've looked at the `WitnessInquisitor.query` and then at its counterpart `Kevery.processQuery` . If I understand the logic correctly ( under the `logs` route), the algorithm doesn't actually return the event that has the given `sn` or the `anchor`. It checks to see if achor can be found and that `sn` is below that of the current state. But then returns the whole KEL anyway:

```for msg in self.db.clonePreIter(pre=pre, fn=0):
                msgs.append(msg)```
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								Or is this wrong?
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								Yes, that is correct.  There is nothing in the request to let the witness know what current state the requestor has and any single key event is useless out of order.  So the safest thing to return is a stream of the full KEL
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-06T21:47:41.000Z
							
							
							    
								Then the response can be received with another `qry` message, `"r":"mbx"` with a `"q":{"topics":{"/receipt"}}` ? Where the actual response will be an SSE event?
								
							
						
						
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-03-06T22:00:51.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	In cases where a signature is attached to a KERI event, the keys used are the latest signing keys for that KEL.  In cases where a signature is attached to something _other than_ a KERI event, it is usually accompanied by a seal in an attachment that specifies the location in the KEL at which is was attached.  This way signatures can last beyond rotations.  If the signature is supposed to be short lived,

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
the "latest" seal can be used to indicate "current key state".

If you are checking for a specific entry in a KEL, you can check your local cache to see if you have _at least_ that sequence number and get the keys from that point in the key state or query one of the AIDs witnesses for a specific sequence number.

If you look at `messagize` in `eventing.py` in KERIpy you can see the application of the two types of Seals mentioned above (inside the `if signers` block).

If you look at `WitnessInquisitor.query` method in `agenting.py` you can see the options for querying a witness for various conditions related to an AID's key state.  For example, you can query by sequence number or by anchor (used for delegation).

  				pfeairheller
            	2023-03-13T21:05:40.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	All interactions between witnesses, agents and a command line like KERIpy should be streamed CESR events.  Since we needed to tunnel that over HTTP, we implemented a quick and dirty method of sending one KERI event (or ACDC credential) at a time over HTTP.  As you can see from Rodo's wireshark capture, it is a simple post that puts the event in the body and all attachments in an HTTP header.  Witnesses and agents will all accept events in this way.

All communication is layered on top of this.  So we have KERI key events, KERI exn events (which may have other events embedded inside them) and ACDC credentials which are still encoded in CESR and thus sent over HTTP in the same manner.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 0
				
  			
		
        
		
			
    			
  				pfeairheller
            	2023-03-13T21:05:58.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	I would consider this method of "tunneling' over HTTP to be crude and something we want to improve on.

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
3-13T21:07:54.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	Communication with the Admin interface for the _current_ Agent (from the keripy `kiwiing` module) is a standard REST API with an incomplete signing mechanism for HTTP requests (this is why we never deployed that agent in any cloud environment).  That is being replaced by a Signify client and a KERIA agent which has a complete KERI signing mechanism for HTTP requests as well as an entire new API which supports _event_ signing at the edge.

vsuvorov

								Perhaps to clarify the need to a formal documentation.

I guess it's an opportunity to also add to the IETF drafts. Specifically, they are missing some of the codes, e.g. `-H` and `-L` in CESR and Proof Signatures respectively ( perhaps it makes sense to reference "extensions" to the tables from the main CESR document).
The events section in the KERI IETF draft could benefit from the examples/explanations that are being created as part of this effort.

Based on my experience, just reading the spec is not enough. I had to do a lot of reverse engineering to actually understand the meaning and usage of the "internal" messages, such as  `qry`, `exn` as well as what various signature attachments mean and how there are used.

I guess, we should view the current *keripy* implementation as the de facto definitive source and update the specs accordingly.  Is this a correct assumption?

In future, an update cycle along with the potential KERI versioning changes can be made to both the ref implementation and the specs. Which would allow implementers to keep up and really position KERI as a standard. Just a thought.

								vsuvorov
								2023-03-15T01:58:19.000Z
							
							
							    
								query messages are used to get values from the Mailboxer.

it works like this (at a high-level):
• To get KEL data, one should send a `qry` message with either `logs` or `ksn` route (in the `r`  key) . 
• `logs` is a stream of KEL data for the prefix in `"q":{"i":"pref"}` part. 
•  `ksn` is the latest key state for the p

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
ref ( same as above)
• These messages will be processed and response will be added to the `mailbox` under a topic. 
    ◦ For `qry` messages the topic is `receipt`
• To get the response, one needs to follow up with another `qry` message, this time with a route set to `mbx` 
    ◦ The system assumes that the wallet or the agent asking it is stateful, but also accommodates a case where some responses were missed, so all the responses for a prefix will be stored along with the number of the response.
    ◦ internally the responses are stored as `"prefix"/"topic"` , that's why topic should be `/"topic"` 
    ◦ the number after a `topic` is the index of the response the agent/wallet is interested in receiving. 
        ▪︎ If there are newer responses under the topic, than the witness will respond will all of them, starting with the requested number. 
        ▪︎ So, if the `qry` has a `topic` value set to `0` then witness will stream back ( via SSE ) all known responses for a `topic`
        ▪︎ Here's an example of a witness receipt response:
```id: 14
event: /receipt
retry: 5000
data: {"v":"KERI10JSON000091_","t":"rct","d":"ELdorFEqtQyGObZRWf54IoKQ5zNdSSA0lvk-VN5lllGs","i":"EAR1eNUllla9Y7l0ru0btqGrFoWuhLk8BkMWFUMEljUY","s":"a"}-CABBEXBtyNmAdUiEMsPYamGdMq4TEQfmitcFAyUYcY15Im20BCMbqyPh0dY2n4AyKw7TX2jsixbqX2nIuNey0QsZmMRTsGMulnjkG76EBYlQdD-q7hgkSSXn_xKhIvUkHB9Ni0H```
where `id: 14` is the index of the response and  `event: /receipt` is the topic  that the `qry` asked for.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-15T01:58:19.000Z
							
							
							    
								The same mechanism is used to provide a "rendezvous" point for communications between agents ( for a challenge message, multisig, etc).

This works via `exn` messages, with the route set to `/fwd`. These messages wrap other messages inside them (under `a` key) and put them into `mailbox` of the corresponding prefix ( `"q":{"pre":"pref"}` under `"q":{"topic":"topic"}` .

Then the wallet of the target pref can get these using the `qry` mechanism described above. Topic can be anything and that's how one can add new type of "peer-to-peer" scenarios.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-15T01:58:19.000Z
							
							
							    
								Some topics are standardised. E.g. all `rct` messages will be posted under the  `receipt` topic. I haven't' gotten to `replay` or `reply` topics yet.  These are related to `Teve

            	query messages are used to get values from the Mailboxer.

it works like this (at a high-level):
• To get KEL data, one should send a `qry` message with either `logs` or `ksn` route (in the `r`  key) . 
• `logs` is a stream of KEL data for the prefix in `"q":{"i":"pref"}` part. 
•  `ksn` is the latest key state for the pref ( same as above)
• These messages will be processed and response will be added to the `mailbox` under a topic. 
    ◦ For `qry` messages the topic is `receipt`
• To get the response, one needs to follow up with another `qry` message, this time with a route set to `mbx` 
    ◦ The system assumes that the wallet or the agent asking it is stateful, but also accommodates a case where some responses were missed, so all the responses for a prefix will be stored along with the number of the response.
    ◦ internally the responses are stored as `"prefix"/"topic"` , that's why topic should be `/"topic"` 
    ◦ the number after a `topic` is the index of the response the agent/wallet is interested in receiving. 
        ▪︎ If there are newer responses under the topic, than the witness will respond will all of them, starting with the requested number. 
        ▪︎ So, if the `qry` has a `topic` value set to `0` then witness will stream back ( via SSE ) all known responses for a `topic`
        ▪︎ Here's an example of a witness receipt response:
id: 14
event: /receipt
retry: 5000
data: {"v":"KERI10JSON000091_","t":"rct","d":"ELdorFEqtQyGObZRWf54IoKQ5zNdSSA0lvk-VN5lllGs","i":"EAR1eNUllla9Y7l0ru0btqGrFoWuhLk8BkMWFUMEljUY","s":"a"}-CABBEXBtyNmAdUiEMsPYamGdMq4TEQfmitcFAyUYcY15Im20BCMbqyPh0dY2n4AyKw7TX2jsixbqX2nIuNey0QsZmMRTsGMulnjkG76EBYlQdD-q7hgkSSXn_xKhIvUkHB9Ni0Hwhere `id: 14` is the index of the response and  `event: /receipt` is the topic  that the

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
e of a server that runs on a Linux VM that will create a local account and populate `authorized_keys` with the public key of any AID that can present a certain credential (any business logic you like around the credential presentation).  Then the Watcher integrated in the server will update the SSH keys on any rotation it detects for any AID that has access.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								So you get enterprise rotatable SSH access via KERI.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								rodolfo.miranda
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Nice trick!
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Same approach could be integrated with something like Active Directory to insert the public keys in there and let Azure manage the access to VMs for you
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								And now imagine if the key that has the credential is a multisig with signing threshold of 1 and rotation threshold of 2 with 3 keys.  You could gain access to SSH from three separate locations and if one become compromised rotate it out and still have access.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								rodolfo.miranda
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								And I just realized that I have ssh keys in many servers, for many years, with no key rotation ever!
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Right???
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								Same here.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								In my humble opinion, the best part is the name...  KERI Authorization for SSH or...  KASSH
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								rodolfo.miranda
								2023-03-16T11:28:13.000Z
							
							
							    
								The security is the security of the weakest point :grinning:

content:
nt protocol URLs for both TCP and HTTP.

They are passed to `self.makeLocScheme`, then `self.reply`, then `self.endorse` of an `eventing.reply` of the following dict:

```{"v":"KERI10JSON0000fe_","t":"rpy","d":"EL8v2q-zLbCqPV4TX2eHLTlhjlcGxb7JUI_33DZo8Zhl","dt":"2023-02-22T11:54:23.468058+00:00","r":"/loc/scheme","a":{"eid":"BDkq35LUU63xnFmfhljYYRY0ymkCg7goyeCxN30tsvmS","scheme":"http","url":""}}-VAi-CABBDkq35LUU63xnFmfhljYYRY0ymkCg7goyeCxN30tsvmS0BA0fNn0QdsoBXG5B2V6_h-dAfVG5cXm6Hg0pZ0mIT-5nHoQKnDTvt6M95hft0ONsMftVZuG9RuYt0jMlvw2Ea0C```
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov

Then you have the prefix: `*B*Dkq35LUU63xnFmfhljYYRY0ymkCg7goyeCxN30tsvmS` and the signature `*0B*A0fNn0QdsoBXG5B2V6_h-dAfVG5cXm6Hg0pZ0mIT-5nHoQKnDTvt6M95hft0ONsMftVZuG9RuYt0jMlvw2Ea0C`
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-03-22T23:38:36.000Z

								vsuvorov
								2023-03-22T23:38:36.000Z

nice work at creating reproducible results
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								kent
								2023-03-23T17:23:36.000Z
							
							
							    
								Yeah, nice work!
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-23T17:23:36.000Z
							
							
							    
								Ok, so this is basically the multisig-agent.sh script changed with the sleeps removed.   Since the creation of the events is asynchronous when witnesses are involved, it is not correct to assume that you can or should be able to immediately query for an OOBI for an event that you just requested through the API.   Your `wait_receipts` has a bug in it in that it can return success when the controller has received all the event receipts but still has not propagated them to the witnesses.   If you really want to wait, you'd have to query all witnesses (or at least the one you want to OOBI against) to make sure they have all the receipts.

The other bug here is that the witness should not be resolving an OOBI if it does not have a threshold satisfying compliment of receipts yet.   It seems to be, under certain circumstances, returning the inception event with only its own receipt.  I've addressed this issue before _somewhere_ but obviously not in OOBI resolution.  If you could create an issue against KERIpy, I'd appreciate it.

As for the excessive CPU, that has been in the TODO list forever.  When we went to production at GLEIF, we metered the escrow processing loop to mitigate the effect of escrowed events on overall performance.  I guess that change never made it back to keripy.  In addition, all events should time out of escrow so the effects should only be transient.  We need to check the timeouts to make sure they are being enforced across all escrows and are of appropriate duration.  I will look at these last two issues this afternoon.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-03-23T17:23:36.000Z
							
							
							    
								I found the resolution to the OOBI resolution problem with `Baser.fullyWitnessed` which is being used in `processQuery`  and `findAnchoringEvent` but not in OOBI resolution.   So in `ending.py` in the `OOBIEnd.on_get` method we just need to change the following:

```        if aid not in self.hby.kevers:
            rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            return```
 to

```        if aid not in self.hby.kevers:

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            return

        kever = self.hby.kevers[aid]
        if not self.hby.db.fullyWitnessed(kever.serder):
            rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            return```
you will get a 404 trying to resolve an OOBI for an AID that is not fully witnessed yet.  Then in your script if you won't want to sleep, you can loop on the OOBI resolution request until it returns a 200.  I'll put together the PR this afternoon, no need to create an issue.

            	Question on `kli`: why a rotate operation for a trans prefix with only one backer returns "ERR: Missing prikey in db for pubkey=D ..." ?  Right after the inception.
            	
			
			
    			Number of replies: 6
				
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								Need more details.  Please open an issue
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								I've decided to debug this a bit, before I open an issue.
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								It looks like there is a race condition:

after successful rotate (`RotateDoer` calls `BaseHab.rotate`),
`indirecting.EventDo`  invokes `msg = self.hab.query(pre=self.pre, src=self.witness, route="mbx", query=q)`
which then causes `BaseHab.endorse` to do `sigers = self.sign(ser=serder.raw, indexed=True)`

`BaseHab.sign` by default has `verfers=None`
which causes it to use `self.kever.verfers`

Th

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
e problem is that at this point in time ( before even the `rot` message has been sent)  `kever.verfers` still has the pre-rotation verkey (the one used for inception), and the `Manager.sign` can't find it anymore, this check fails:
```if ((signer := self.ks.pris.get(verfer.qb64,
                                                decrypter=self.decrypter))
                        is None):
                    raise ValueError("Missing prikey in db for pubkey={}".format(verfer.qb64))```
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								`Manager.rotate` has already erased the inception's verkey:
```if erase:
            for pub in old.pubs:  # remove prior old prikeys not current old
                self.ks.pris.rem(pub)```
`erase` is by default `True`
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								vsuvorov
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								Debugging asynchronous code is fun :)
								
							
						
						
						
							
								
								pfeairheller
								2023-04-03T21:33:26.000Z
							
							
							    
								I feel that in my soul!

            	It looks like there is a race condition:

after successful rotate (`RotateDoer` calls `BaseHab.rotate`),
`indirecting.EventDo`  invokes `msg = self.hab.query(pre=self.pre, src=self.witness, route="mbx", query=q)`
which then causes `BaseHab.endorse` to do `sigers = self.sign(ser=serder.raw, indexed=True)`

`BaseHab.sign` by default has `verfers=None`
which causes it to use `self.kever.verfers`

The problem is that at this point in time ( before even the `rot` message has been sent)  `kever.verfers` still has the pre-rotation verkey (the one used for inception), and the `Manager.sign` can't find it anymore, this check fails:

            	Question/observation re. connection handling for `mbx`  `qry` messages sent to the witness.

The server responds with a header: `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`  and then sends one or more SSEs in a chunk.  But it doesn't add last chunk (`0CRLF`) , which causes an off the shelf HTTP1 client to time out.

Considering another header, `Connection: close`, the desired behavior is that the client closes the connection after the chunk with the SSEs is received. Correct? Just making sure the intent is not to have the client stay online and listen to more incoming SSEs.

Shouldn't the server close the connection itself or at least send the "last chunk" sequence, though?

3. client will reconnect to the same uri with a delay as per `retry` field
    a. server will return error (404) if the query has timed out in the cache and is no longer findable.

Currently, the `qry` message includes a topic along with it's index, which will be used to provide either the last response if the index matches, or all the responses starting from the specified index, if the index is not the last one. After digging around the code and playing with various query approaches, my impression is that the signed `qry` message enforces a certain amount of trust establishment between witnesses and external controllers/agents (prefix and it's backers have to be known to a witness). There's also a special use-case for `exn` forward messages where a response to a `qry` coming (signed by)  from a prefix which belongs to another known witness/backer will be sent to one of the backers of that prefix.             	Ok, so this is basically the multisig-agent.sh script changed with the sleeps removed.   Since the creation of the events is asynchronous when witnesses are involved, it is not correct to assume that you can or should be able to immediately query for an OOBI for an event that you just requested through the API.   Your `wait_receipts` has a bug in it in that it can return success when the controller has received all the event receipts but still has not propagated them to the witnesses.   If you really want to wait, you'd have to query all witnesses (or at least the one you want to OOBI against) to make sure they have all the receipts.

The other bug here is that the witness should not be resolving an OOBI if it does not have a threshold satisfying compliment of receipts yet.   It seems to be, under certain circumstances, returning the inception event with only its own receipt.  I've addressed this issue before _somewhere_ but obviously not in OOBI resolution.  If you could create an issue against KERIpy,

author: Slack Keri Members
content:
I'd appreciate it.

As for the excessive CPU, that has been in the TODO list forever.  When we went to production at GLEIF, we metered the escrow processing loop to mitigate the effect of escrowed events on overall performance.  I guess that change never made it back to keripy.  In addition, all events should time out of escrow so the effects should only be transient.  We need to check the timeouts to make sure they are being enforced across all escrows and are of appropriate duration.  I will look at these last two issues this afternoon.

  				pfeairheller
            	2023-04-12T16:07:36.000Z
			
			
  			    
            	I found the resolution to the OOBI resolution problem with `Baser.fullyWitnessed` which is being used in `processQuery`  and `findAnchoringEvent` but not in OOBI resolution.   So in `ending.py` in the `OOBIEnd.on_get` method we just need to change the following:

        if aid not in self.hby.kevers:
            rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            return to

        if aid not in self.hby.kevers:
            rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            return

        kever = self.hby.kevers[aid]
        if not self.hby.db.fullyWitnessed(kever.serder):
            rep.status = falcon.HTTP_NOT_FOUND
            returnyou will get a 404 trying to resolve an OOBI for an AID that is not fully witnessed yet.  Then in your script if you won't want to sleep, you can loop on the OOBI resolution request until it returns a 200.  I'll put together the PR this afternoon, no need to create an issue.

kli incept --name delegate2 --alias delegate2 --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/delegate-2.json

kli incept --name delegator1 --alias delegator1 --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/delegator-1.json

echo "Creating delegator's second local identifier in delegator2 keystore"

IPT_DIR}/data/delegator-2.json


echo "Sharing OOBIs between delegate's two local identifiers"

echo "Sharing OOBIs between delegator's two local identifiers"
kli oobi resolve --name delegator1 --oobi-alias delegator2 --oobi

# In 2 delegator terminal windows run the following
kli multisig incept --name delegator1 --alias delegator1 --group delegator --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/multisig-delegator.json &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

kli multisig incept --name delegator2 --alias delegator2 --group delegator --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/multisig-delegator.json &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

# Wait for the multisig delegator to be created
wait $PID_LIST

# Delegator does not need an oobi for delegate.
kli oobi resolve --name delegate1 --oobi-alias delegator --oobi 
kli oobi resolve --name delegate2 --oobi-alias delegator --oobi 

# Run the delegate commands in parallel so they can collaborate and request delegation
kli multisig incept --name delegate1 --alias delegate1 --group delegate --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/multisig-delegate.json &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

kli multisig incept --name delegate2 --alias delegate2 --group delegate --file ${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/multisig-delegate.json &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

# Wait for 3 seconds to allow the delegation request to complete and then launch the approval in parallel
sleep 3

kli delegate confirm --name delegator1 --alias delegator --interact --auto &
#kli multisig interact --name delegator1 --alias delegator --data @${KERI_DEMO_SCRIPT_DIR}/data/multisig-delegate-icp-anchor.json &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

PID_LIST+=" $pid"

wait $PID_LIST

pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"
kli multisig rotate --name delegate2 --alias delegate &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

kli delegate confirm --name delegator1 --alias delegator --interact --auto &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"
kli delegate confirm --name delegator2 --alias delegator --interact --auto &
pid=$!
PID_LIST+=" $pid"

sleep 5

kli multisig continue --name delegate1 --alias delegate
